Origins of Life on Earth
Proper chromosome separation produces haploid gametes with maternal and paternal chromosomes.
Water's polarity and hydrogen bonding enable temperature regulation in organisms.
C, H, O form most biomolecules; S, P, N serve specialized roles.
Hydrolysis breaks polymer bonds by adding water molecules to monomers.
Simple sugar monomers link via covalent bonds forming linear or branched polysaccharides.
Lipids are nonpolar hydrophobic molecules; saturation determines chain structure and physical state.
Nucleotides are monomers with sugar, phosphate, and nitrogenous base components.
Peptide bonds link amino acids into linear protein chains.
Ribosomes, made of rRNA and protein, synthesize proteins from mRNA
Surface area-to-volume ratios determine cellular exchange efficiency with environment.
Phospholipids have polar heads facing water and nonpolar tails facing inward.
Hydrophobic membrane interior enables selective permeability between cellular environments.
Membrane selectivity enables solute concentration gradients across membranes.
Channel proteins enable charged ions to cross membranes, causing polarization.
Water moves osmotically from high to low water potential across concentration gradients.
ATP powers active transport and electrochemical gradient maintenance via membrane proteins.
Eukaryotic membranes and organelles separate metabolic processes and enzymatic reactions.
Organelles evolved from free-living prokaryotes through endosymbiosis.
Enzymes are protein catalysts that regulate biological processes by lowering activation energy.
Enzyme structural changes from temperature, pH, or chemicals reduce catalytic function.
Living organisms need continuous energy input to function.
Photosynthesis converts light, CO₂, and water into carbohydrates and oxygen; originated in prokaryotes.
Cellular respiration synthesizes ATP from biological macromolecules universally.
Cells communicate through direct contact or distant chemical signaling.
Signal transduction pathways connect signal reception to cellular responses.
Signal transduction causes gene expression and cell function changes, altering phenotype or triggering apoptosis.
Negative feedback reduces stimuli to restore set point; positive feedback amplifies responses.
Cell cycle is regulated progression through interphase stages, mitosis, and cytokinesis with G0 exit option.
Internal checkpoints control cell cycle progression through multiple regulatory points.
Meiosis produces haploid gamete cells in sexually reproducing diploid organisms.
Mendel's laws apply to genes located on different chromosomes.
Non-Mendelian inheritance patterns include genetic linkage, codominance, and incomplete dominance.
Same genotype produces different phenotypes under varying environmental conditions.
DNA and RNA store and transmit genetic information across generations; prokaryotes use circular chromosomes, eukaryotes use linear chromosomes condensed with histones.
DNA replication is semiconservative; enzymes unwind, prime, synthesize, and ligate strands.
RNA sequence and structure determine function through mRNA, tRNA, and rRNA roles.
mRNA translation to polypeptides occurs on ribosomes in cytoplasm and rough ER.
Multiple scientific disciplines provide evidence supporting evolution.
DNA regulatory sequences control transcription through protein interactions.
Transcription factors and RNA polymerase bind DNA sequences to initiate transcription.
Mutations alter DNA sequences, affecting protein type, amount, and phenotype.
Genetic engineering techniques analyze and manipulate DNA through electrophoresis, PCR, transformation, and sequencing.
Natural selection represents a primary evolutionary mechanism driving biological change.
Natural selection operates on phenotypic variation present in populations.
Humans use artificial selection to alter genetic variation within populations.
Random processes including mutation, drift, bottleneck, founder effect, and migration alter allele frequencies.
Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium models allele frequencies under ideal non-evolving conditions.
Shared cellular and molecular structures demonstrate evolutionary common ancestry among eukaryotes.
All species evolve continuously through genomic changes, fossil record shifts, and adaptive resistance.
Phylogenetic trees and cladograms depict testable evolutionary relationships.
Genetic diversity enables populations to adapt and survive environmental changes and pressures.
Geological evidence supports life's origin; Earth formed 4.6 bya, habitable 3.9 bya, fossils 3.5 bya.
Organisms use behavioral and physiological responses to environmental changes.
Organisms use energy for growth, reproduction, and homeostasis through temperature regulation.
Populations are same-species organisms interacting complexly with each other and environment.
Carrying capacity represents maximum sustainable population size based on available resources.
Community structure depends on species composition and diversity measured quantitatively.
Low diversity ecosystems are less resilient to environmental changes.
Adaptations are selected genetic variations conferring environmental advantages.
Density-dependent and density-independent limiting factors produce logistic population growth modeled mathematically.
Signal transduction pathways use protein modifications and phosphorylation cascades.
Stroma contains Calvin cycle reactions; thylakoids in grana contain light reactions and photosystems.
Eukaryotic aerobic respiration uses coordinated enzyme reactions capturing energy.
Local regulators enable short-distance cell-to-cell communication in nearby vicinity.
Mutations in signaling pathway components alter downstream responses and cellular outcomes.
Mitosis ensures equal genome distribution through prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase, and cytokinesis.
Cyclin and cyclin-dependent kinase interactions regulate cell cycle progression.
Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes, producing two haploid daughter cells.
Hydrogen bonds between water molecules create cohesion, adhesion, surface tension.
Crossing over during prophase I exchanges genetic material between non-sister chromatids.
RNA world hypothesis: RNA was earliest genetic material; replicated via base-pairing without protein catalysts.
Fertilization creates genetic variation through new allele combinations; probability rules and crosses determine dominance patterns and predict inheritance.
Sex-linked traits on sex chromosomes show predictable inheritance patterns differing by organism.
Organisms exchange information responding to internal and external cues altering behavior.
Both prokaryotes and eukaryotes may contain plasmids, extra-chromosomal circular DNA molecules.
Keystone species, producers, and essential factors maintain ecosystem diversity.
RNA polymerase uses template DNA strand to synthesize new RNA molecules.
Organisms adapt reproductive strategies based on available energy resources.
Prokaryotic translation occurs simultaneously with transcription of mRNA.
Reversible DNA and histone modifications alter gene expression without changing sequence.
Negative regulators block transcription by binding to DNA sequences.
DNA replication errors and external factors cause random mutations providing variation.
Competition for resources causes differential survival; favorable traits increase reproductive success across generations.
Changing environments exert selective pressures on populations.
Dehydration synthesis joins monomers by removing water, forming polymers.
Adaptations reflect energy/matter acquisition; growth depends on birth, death rates; unconstrained reproduction causes exponential growth.
Lipids function in energy storage, insulation, hormone signaling, membrane structure, and homeostasis.
Nucleic acids have 5' and 3' ends; synthesis adds nucleotides to 3' end.
Random processes drive allele frequency changes through mutation, drift, and gene flow interactions.
R group properties determine local protein structure and function.
Endomembrane system modifies, packages, and transports cellular molecules
Organisms maintain order by coupling energy-releasing and energy-requiring processes.
Cell size, shape, and metabolic demands are constrained by surface-to-volume ratios.
Calculate allele frequencies from genotype frequencies using Hardy–Weinberg equation.
Membrane proteins have hydrophilic regions facing water and hydrophobic regions contacting lipids.
Fossil dating and morphological homologies reveal evolutionary change and common ancestry.
Nonpolar molecules cross freely; polar molecules require transport proteins.
Passive transport moves molecules down gradients without energy input.
Heterozygotes sometimes have higher fitness than either homozygote.
Large polar molecules move passively across membranes down concentration gradients.
Communities consist of interacting species populations that change dynamically.
Continuous molecular movement across membranes maintains growth and homeostasis.
Trees show temporal change; cladograms lack time scale and evolutionary distance.
Internal membranes reduce competing reactions and expand surface area for catalysis.
Prokaryotes lack membrane organelles but possess specialized internal regions.
Substrate shape and charge must match enzyme active site for reaction to occur.
Some denatured enzymes can recover their catalytic activity when conditions normalize.
Eukaryotes use internal membranes to create specialized cellular compartments.
DNA double helix has antiparallel strands with A-T and C-G base pairs.
Sequential metabolic pathways control energy transfer through product-to-reactant linkage.
Organisms communicate via multiple signal types affecting other organisms' behavior and reproductive success.
ER provides support and transport; rough ER synthesizes proteins, smooth ER detoxifies
Gained/lost traits construct trees; shared derived characters and molecular data are most informative.
Gene regulation causes differential expression affecting cell products and functions.
Light reactions produce ATP and NADPH powering Calvin cycle and cellular metabolism.
Specific base pairing (purines with pyrimidines) is conserved in nucleic acids; adenine pairs with thymine or uracil, guanine pairs with cytosine.
Meiosis II separates sister chromatids, producing four haploid daughter cells.
Cell cycle disruptions lead to cancer development or programmed cell death.
Aquaporin proteins facilitate rapid water transport through membranes.
ETC establishes gradients; chemiosmosis drives ATP synthesis and heat production.
One gene can control multiple traits, preventing independent segregation.
Allele frequency changes demonstrate that evolution occurs within populations.
Chemical interactions with pathway components activate or inhibit signal transduction.
Receptors recognize ligands; located on surface or internally; G-protein coupled receptors exemplified.
Hormonal signals travel long distances between different cell types.
Amino acid sequence determines primary structure and overall shape.
Phospholipid tails block ions and polar molecules; small polar molecules pass minimally.
Mitosis and meiosis errors cause chromosome number and structure changes affecting phenotype.
Membranes are fluid mosaics of phospholipids, proteins, steroids, and carbohydrates.
Mutations occur randomly, not in response to environmental needs.
Sexual reproduction increases genetic variation through crossing over, chromosome assortment, and fertilization.
Ecological organization progresses from populations to biomes hierarchically.
Fitness quantifies an organism's reproductive output and genetic contribution to future populations.
Translation involves initiation, elongation, and termination with codons specifying amino acids via tRNA.
Keystone species have disproportionate effects; their removal causes ecosystem collapse.
Population interactions regulate access to energy and matter.
DNA and protein sequence comparisons demonstrate evolutionary relationships and descent.
Phenotypic variations affect organism fitness in specific environments.
RNA polymerase synthesizes mRNA 5' to 3' while reading template 3' to 5'.
Substrate and product concentrations control the efficiency of enzymatic reactions.
Active transport uses energy to move molecules against gradients.
Phenotype depends on which genes express and at what levels they're expressed.
Osmoregulation controls water balance and internal solute composition through water movement.
Molecular variation increases population survival and reproduction in diverse environments.
Eukaryotic mRNA undergoes poly-A tail, GTP cap addition, and alternative splicing.
Retroviruses use reverse transcriptase to convert RNA to DNA for integration and expression.
Energy flows one-way; matter cycles between organisms and environment interdependently.
Prokaryotic operons and eukaryotic genes coordinate regulation via shared mechanisms.
Small RNA molecules regulate gene expression.
Genotype changes affect phenotypes subject to natural selection and survival advantages.
Cell walls provide structural support, permeability regulation, and osmotic protection.
Environmental changes alter selection pressures, causing varied genetic variants to be favored each generation.
Hydrogen bonding forms alpha-helices and beta-pleated sheets.
Higher temperatures increase enzyme-substrate collision frequency, accelerating reactions optimally.
Information exchange drives evolution; innate and learned behaviors plus cooperation enhance fitness.
Endocytosis and exocytosis use energy for large molecule transport via vesicles.
Invasive species exploit vacant niches or outcompete natives for resources.
Fundamental metabolic pathways are shared across all three domains of life.
DNA and RNA differ in sugar, bases, and strand structure.
Trees illustrate speciation; nodes represent most recent common ancestors of groups.
Thylakoid electron transport chain transfers electrons to NADP⁺ forming NADPH in photosystem I.
Glycolysis converts glucose to ATP, NADH, and pyruvate molecules.
Cascades amplify signals through shape changes, enzymes, second messengers, hormones, and ion channels.
Population relationships show positive/negative effects; examples: predation, cooperation, trophic cascades, niche partitioning.
Mitosis and meiosis both use spindle apparatus but differ in cell number and genetic content.
Golgi folds, modifies, and packages proteins for distribution
Organellar DNA inheritance is non-nuclear and typically maternal in both animals and plants.
Multiple interactions create three-dimensional tertiary protein structure.
Double membrane mitochondria compartmentalize aerobic respiration and ATP synthesis
Competition, predation, and symbiotic interactions drive population changes.
Inhibitors reduce enzyme activity by binding to active or allosteric sites.
Chlorophylls absorb light energy; water splits to replace electrons in photosystem II.
Biogeochemical cycles involve abiotic/biotic reservoirs and processes cycling matter.
Trees constructed from morphological or molecular sequence data similarities.
Pyruvate oxidation in Krebs cycle reduces NAD+ and FAD, releases CO2.
Trees represent testable hypotheses revised continuously with new evidence.
Krebs cycle in matrix produces ATP, CO2, and electron carriers.
Multiple polypeptides interact; all structural levels determine function.
Water cycle circulates through oceans, atmosphere, and organisms via evaporation and precipitation.
Lysosomes digest material and participate in apoptosis
Speciation occurs when populations become reproductively isolated from each other.
Carbon cycles through photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, and combustion processes.
Vacuoles store materials; plant vacuoles maintain turgor pressure
Glycolysis and Krebs cycle electrons feed into electron transport chain.
Nitrogen cycles through fixation, assimilation, and denitrification via soil microorganisms.
Double membrane chloroplasts in plants conduct photosynthesis
Biological species concept defines species by reproductive compatibility and genetic exchange.
Punctuated equilibrium: rapid change after stasis; gradualism: slow, continuous change.
Phosphorus cycles from rock weathering through organisms back to soil via decomposition.
Divergent evolution produces phenotypic diversity; adaptive radiation accelerates during habitat availability.
Convergent evolution produces similar phenotypes from similar selective pressures.
Sympatric speciation occurs when populations overlap geographically.
